{{ill|Historic Site of Japan|WD=Q30834580}}; {{ill|historic site|WD=Q1081138}}| website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }}{{OSM location map | coord = {{Coord|34|56|28|N|133|37|43|E}} | zoom = 15 | float = right | width = 300| height =250 | caption = {{center|大谷・定古墳群分布図}} | scalemark = 60 | mark-coord1= {{Coord|34|56|29.37|N|133|37|52.90|E}} | label1=定東塚古墳 | label-pos1=right | label-color1=black | mark1=方墳.png | mark-size1=25 | mark-coord2= {{Coord|34|56|29.30|N|133|37|52.00|E}} | label2=定西塚古墳 | label-pos2=left | label-color2=black | mark2=方墳.png | mark-size2=25 | mark-coord3= {{Coord|34|56|31.45|N|133|37|52.52|E}} | label3=定北古墳 | label-pos3=top | label-color3=black | mark3=方墳.png | mark-size3=25 | mark-coord4= {{Coord|34|56|22.15|N|133|37|29.25|E}} | label4=大谷1号墳 | label-pos4=top | label-color4=black | mark4=方墳.png | mark-size4=25 }} [[File:Sada Higashizuka Kofun and Sada Nishizuka Kofun, funkyu.jpg|thumb|270px|right|Sada-Higashi and Sada-Nishi Kofun]] [[File:Sada Kita Kofun, funkyu-2.jpg|270px|right|thumb|Sada-Kita Kofun]] The {{nihongo|'''Ōya-Sada Kofun Cluster'''|大谷・定古墳群| Ōya-Sada Kofun-gun|}} is a group of six ''[[kofun]]'' [[Kofun]]s located in the Kaminakatsui neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Maniwa, Okayama|Maniwa]], [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]], in the [[:en:San'yō region|San'yō region]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The tumuli were collectively designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 2008.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/140557|title=大谷・定古墳群|trans-title=Ōya-Sada Kofun-gun|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 31, 2022}}</ref> The tumuli area considered to be representative of large chieftain tombs of the late Kofun period of the [[Kingdom of Kibi]].
==Background== The Ōya-Sada Kofun Cluster is located by the Nakatsui River, which flows through a basin surrounded by the Kibi Plateau, in southern Maniwa. A common feature is that all are {{nihongo|''{{ill|Square Kofun|lt=hōfun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}''|方墳|1=Square Kofun||}}-style rectangular Kofuns, with a stepped (pyramid) structure and a stone wall layer of outer stones.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
Ōya No.1 Kofun was built in the latter half of the 7th century. [[Archaeological excavation]]s have unearthed gold-decorated iron swords and other items from the cut stone [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]]. It is believed to date from the late 7th century. The Sada Kofun cluster is located at the foot of the mountain 1.5 km east of the Ōya No. 1 Kofun, and consist of five tumuli. The Sada-Higashi and Sada-Nishi tumuli were once regarded as a single "two conjoined rectangles" type ({{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|lt=zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|lt=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/zenpō-kōhō-fun|13=User:Immanuelle/zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (前方後方墳)); however, subsequent research indicated that they are separate tumuli.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
;Sada-Higashi Kofun The Sada-Higashi Kofun is rectangular, measuring 25 meters north to south, 18 meters east to west, and 6 meters high. The mound is surrounded by two tiers of stones. Excavations were conducted from 1994 to 1997. The burial facility is a horizontal stone chamber orientated to the south. The total length of the burial chamber is 11.6 meters including the entry passage. Inside were four [[:en:Haji pottery|Haji-style ceramic]] coffins (2 tortoise shell-shaped, 2 gabled house-shaped, 1 four-pillar house-shaped) and a nailed wooden coffin. Grave goods included cearrings, small beads, gold thread iron long swords, arrowheads, halberds, and metal fittings, horse harness and metal fittings, agricultural tools and [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] pottery (bowls, lids, vases, jars). It is estimated to have been built around the end of the Kofun period, around the first half of the 7th century, and is the oldest among the cluster of ''kofun''.
;Sada-Nishi Kofu The Sada-Nishi Kofun was built adjacent to the west side of the Sada-Higashi Kofun on the southern foot of a hill overhanging the east bank of the Nakatsui River. The mound is rectangular, measuring 16 meters north to south, 14 meters east to west, and 4.5 meters high and is surrounded by two tiers of stones. Excavations were conducted in 1994–1997. The burial facility is a horizontal stone chamber orientated to the south. The total length of the burial chamber including the entry passage was 10.7 meters. Inside were six Haji pottery coffins (2 tortoiseshell-shaped, 4 gabled house-shaped) and nailed wooden coffins. Grave goods were similar to that of the Sada-Higashi Kofun, and date the construction to the end of the Kofun period, around the first half of the 7th century, and immediately following the Sada-Higashi Kofun.
;Sada-Kita Kofun The Sada-Kita Kofun is located about 70 meters north of the Sada-Higashi Kofun and Sada-Nishi Kofun on the southern slope of a hill overhanging the east bank of the Nakatsui River. The mound is rectangular, measuring 25.3 meters from north to south and 21 meters from east to west, and is surrounded by three tiers of outer guard stones; the middle tier measuring 1.8 meters contains the opening of the burial chamber. Excavations were conducted from 1990 to 1993. The burial facility is a horizontal stone chamber orientated to the south. The chamber is two-tiered and is paved with flat pebbles. Inside were four Haji pottery coffins (2 tortoiseshell-shaped, 2 gabled house-shaped), wooden coffins with nails (1.8m in length, 0.6m in width), and an additional burial urn (medicine pot-shaped Sue pottery). Grave goods included small ball and cut-glass beads, weapons (iron swords, arrowheads), copper bowls, ax-shaped iron products, and Sue pottery (bowls, lids, and flat jars). Sue pottery jars have also been unearthed on both sides of the opening. The tumulus is estimated to have been built around the middle of the 7th century, at the end of the Kofun period.
;Ōya No.1 Kofun The Ōya No.1 Kofun was built on the southern slope of a hill overhanging the west bank of the Nakatsui River. The mound is rectangular, measuring 13.1 meters east–west, 9.8 meters north–south, and 3.8 meters high. It is surrounded by three tiers of stone guards, and two tiers of stones (18–22 meters in length) in front of the mound, giving the front view the appearance of a five-tier construction.The top of the mound is covered with flat stones. Excavations were conducted in 1988 and from 1992 to 1994. The burial facility is a horizontal stone chamber with two sleeves and opens in the south-southeast direction. The burial chamber was made from five megalithic stones, one each on the back wall, east wall, and ceiling, and two on the west wall. Inside was a Sue pottery ceramic coffin shaped like a house and a wooden coffin. Although the burial chamber had been robbed in antiquity, a number of grave goods were found, including a pair of dragon hilt swords, arrowheads, and arrowhead-shaped gilt bronze products and both Sue pottery and Haji pottery pottery. The excavated hilt swords are an important cultural property designated by Okayama Prefecture.<ref name="金銅装環頭大刀">[https://www.pref.okayama.jp/uploaded/attachment/261938.pdf 金銅装環頭大刀]（岡山県ホームページ）</ref> The tumulus is estimated to have been built around the end of the Kofun period around the latter half of the 7th century. Although it is not clear who was buried, there is a theory that he was Prince Ishikawa no Okimi, who died in 672, who played a role in the [[:en:Jinshin War|Jinshin War]] and was recognized as ruler of Kibi around this time.
;Sada No.4 Kofun The Sada No.4 Kofun was excavated in 2006. Measuring approximately 6.6 meters from north to south and 8.2 meters from east to west, it is a three-tiered square tumulus. No relics have been found in the stone chamber, but plate-shaped iron products have been excavated from the southern end of the research area. It is estimated to have been built between the end of the 7th century and the beginning of the 8th century, the end of the Kofun period, making it the newest and smallest Kofun in the cluster.
;Sada No.5 Kofun The Sada No.5 Kofun was also excavated in 2006. It is a two-tiered square Kofun measuring about 8 meters from north to south and about 10.4 meters from east to west. Sue pottery was found in the burial chamber. It is estimated to have been built in the latter half of the 7th century, at the end of the Kofun period.
Large chieftain tombs of the 6th century Kingdom of Kibi were built in the southern part of the Kibi region, but the northern region, which is an important transportation hub and rich in iron resources, came under Yamato political influence in the 7th century and the construction of large Kofuns in this region shows a shifting of political and economic power from the south to the north during this time. In addition, the Kodono Site, believed to be the ruins of a [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] ''kanga'' (district headquarters) are located nearby, and it ay have some connection to this tumuli cluster.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The site is about ten minutes by car from [[:en:Chugoku Expressway|Chugoku Expressway]] Hokubo IC.

The mound is rectangular, measuring 25.3 meters from north to south and 21 meters from east to west, and is surrounded by three tiers of outer guard stones; the middle tier measuring 1.8 meters contains the opening of the burial chamber. Excavations were conducted from 1990 to 1993. The burial facility is a horizontal stone chamber orientated to the south. The chamber is two-tiered and is paved with flat pebbles. Inside were four Haji pottery coffins (2 tortoiseshell-shaped, 2 gabled house-shaped), wooden coffins with nails (1.8m in length, 0.6m in width), and an additional burial urn (medicine pot-shaped Sue pottery). Grave goods included small ball and cut-glass beads, weapons (iron swords, arrowheads), copper bowls, ax-shaped iron products, and Sue pottery (bowls, lids, and flat jars). Sue pottery jars have also been unearthed on both sides of the opening. The tumulus is estimated to have been built around the middle of the 7th century, at the end of the Kofun period.
;Ōya No.1 Kofun The Ōya No.1 Kofun was built on the southern slope of a hill overhanging the west bank of the Nakatsui River. The mound is rectangular, measuring 13.1 meters east–west, 9.8 meters north–south, and 3.8 meters high. It is surrounded by three tiers of stone guards, and two tiers of stones (18–22 meters in length) in front of the mound, giving the front view the appearance of a five-tier construction.The top of the mound is covered with flat stones. Excavations were conducted in 1988 and from 1992 to 1994. The burial facility is a horizontal stone chamber with two sleeves and opens in the south-southeast direction. The burial chamber was made from five megalithic stones, one each on the back wall, east wall, and ceiling, and two on the west wall. Inside was a Sue pottery ceramic coffin shaped like a house and a wooden coffin. Although the burial chamber had been robbed in antiquity, a number of grave goods were found, including a pair of dragon hilt swords, arrowheads, and arrowhead-shaped gilt bronze products and both Sue pottery and Haji pottery pottery. The excavated hilt swords are an important cultural property designated by Okayama Prefecture.<ref name="金銅装環頭大刀">[https://www.pref.okayama.jp/uploaded/attachment/261938.pdf 金銅装環頭大刀]（岡山県ホームページ）</ref> The tumulus is estimated to have been built around the end of the Kofun period around the latter half of the 7th century. Although it is not clear who was buried, there is a theory that he was Prince Ishikawa no Okimi, who died in 672, who played a role in the [[:en:Jinshin War|Jinshin War]] and was recognized as ruler of Kibi around this time.
;Sada No.4 Kofun The Sada No.4 Kofun was excavated in 2006. Measuring approximately 6.6 meters from north to south and 8.2 meters from east to west, it is a three-tiered square tumulus. No relics have been found in the stone chamber, but plate-shaped iron products have been excavated from the southern end of the research area. It is estimated to have been built between the end of the 7th century and the beginning of the 8th century, the end of the Kofun period, making it the newest and smallest Kofun in the cluster.
;Sada No.5 Kofun The Sada No.5 Kofun was also excavated in 2006. It is a two-tiered square Kofun measuring about 8 meters from north to south and about 10.4 meters from east to west. Sue pottery was found in the burial chamber. It is estimated to have been built in the latter half of the 7th century, at the end of the Kofun period.
Large chieftain tombs of the 6th century Kingdom of Kibi were built in the southern part of the Kibi region, but the northern region, which is an important transportation hub and rich in iron resources, came under Yamato political influence in the 7th century and the construction of large Kofuns in this region shows a shifting of political and economic power from the south to the north during this time. In addition, the Kodono Site, believed to be the ruins of a [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] ''kanga'' (district headquarters) are located nearby, and it ay have some connection to this tumuli cluster.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The site is about ten minutes by car from [[:en:Chugoku Expressway|Chugoku Expressway]] Hokubo IC.

{{ill|Historic Site of Japan|WD=Q30834580}}; {{ill|historic site|WD=Q1081138}}| website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }}{{OSM location map | coord = {{Coord|34|56|28|N|133|37|43|E}} | zoom = 15 | float = right | width = 300| height =250 | caption = {{center|大谷・定古墳群分布図}} | scalemark = 60 | mark-coord1= {{Coord|34|56|29.37|N|133|37|52.90|E}} | label1=定東塚古墳 | label-pos1=right | label-color1=black | mark1=方墳.png | mark-size1=25 | mark-coord2= {{Coord|34|56|29.30|N|133|37|52.00|E}} | label2=定西塚古墳 | label-pos2=left | label-color2=black | mark2=方墳.png | mark-size2=25 | mark-coord3= {{Coord|34|56|31.45|N|133|37|52.52|E}} | label3=定北古墳 | label-pos3=top | label-color3=black | mark3=方墳.png | mark-size3=25 | mark-coord4= {{Coord|34|56|22.15|N|133|37|29.25|E}} | label4=大谷1号墳 | label-pos4=top | label-color4=black | mark4=方墳.png | mark-size4=25 }} [[File:Sada Higashizuka Kofun and Sada Nishizuka Kofun, funkyu.jpg|thumb|270px|right|Sada-Higashi and Sada-Nishi Kofun]] [[File:Sada Kita Kofun, funkyu-2.jpg|270px|right|thumb|Sada-Kita Kofun]] The {{nihongo|'''Ōya-Sada Kofun Cluster'''|大谷・定古墳群| Ōya-Sada Kofun-gun|}} is a group of six ''[[kofun]]'' [[Kofun]]s located in the Kaminakatsui neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Maniwa, Okayama|Maniwa]], {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|en|Okayama Prefecture|WD=Q132936|lt=Okayama Prefecture}}, in the {{ill|San'yō region|en|San'yō region|WD=Q398087|lt=San'yō region}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. The tumuli were collectively designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 2008.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/140557|title=大谷・定古墳群|trans-title=Ōya-Sada Kofun-gun|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 31, 2022}}</ref> The tumuli area considered to be representative of large chieftain tombs of the late Kofun period of the [[Kingdom of Kibi]].
==Background== The Ōya-Sada Kofun Cluster is located by the Nakatsui River, which flows through a basin surrounded by the Kibi Plateau, in southern Maniwa. A common feature is that all are {{nihongo|''{{ill|Square Kofun|lt=hōfun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}''|方墳|1=Square Kofun||}}-style rectangular Kofuns, with a stepped (pyramid) structure and a stone wall layer of outer stones.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
Ōya No.1 Kofun was built in the latter half of the 7th century. [[Archaeological excavation]]s have unearthed gold-decorated iron swords and other items from the cut stone [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]]. It is believed to date from the late 7th century. The Sada Kofun cluster is located at the foot of the mountain 1.5 km east of the Ōya No. 1 Kofun, and consist of five tumuli. The Sada-Higashi and Sada-Nishi tumuli were once regarded as a single "two conjoined rectangles" type ({{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|lt=zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|lt=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/zenpō-kōhō-fun|13=User:Immanuelle/zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (前方後方墳)); however, subsequent research indicated that they are separate tumuli.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
;Sada-Higashi Kofun The Sada-Higashi Kofun is rectangular, measuring 25 meters north to south, 18 meters east to west, and 6 meters high. The mound is surrounded by two tiers of stones. Excavations were conducted from 1994 to 1997. The burial facility is a horizontal stone chamber orientated to the south. The total length of the burial chamber is 11.6 meters including the entry passage. Inside were four {{ill|Haji pottery|en|Haji pottery|WD=Q5639938|lt=Haji-style ceramic}} coffins (2 tortoise shell-shaped, 2 gabled house-shaped, 1 four-pillar house-shaped) and a nailed wooden coffin. Grave goods included cearrings, small beads, gold thread iron long swords, arrowheads, halberds, and metal fittings, horse harness and metal fittings, agricultural tools and {{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|WD=Q7634351|lt=Sue pottery}} pottery (bowls, lids, vases, jars). It is estimated to have been built around the end of the Kofun period, around the first half of the 7th century, and is the oldest among the cluster of ''kofun''.
;Sada-Nishi Kofu The Sada-Nishi Kofun was built adjacent to the west side of the Sada-Higashi Kofun on the southern foot of a hill overhanging the east bank of the Nakatsui River. The mound is rectangular, measuring 16 meters north to south, 14 meters east to west, and 4.5 meters high and is surrounded by two tiers of stones. Excavations were conducted in 1994–1997. The burial facility is a horizontal stone chamber orientated to the south. The total length of the burial chamber including the entry passage was 10.7 meters. Inside were six Haji pottery coffins (2 tortoiseshell-shaped, 4 gabled house-shaped) and nailed wooden coffins. Grave goods were similar to that of the Sada-Higashi Kofun, and date the construction to the end of the Kofun period, around the first half of the 7th century, and immediately following the Sada-Higashi Kofun.
;Sada-Kita Kofun The Sada-Kita Kofun is located about 70 meters north of the Sada-Higashi Kofun and Sada-Nishi Kofun on the southern slope of a hill overhanging the east bank of the Nakatsui River. The mound is rectangular, measuring 25.3 meters from north to south and 21 meters from east to west, and is surrounded by three tiers of outer guard stones; the middle tier measuring 1.8 meters contains the opening of the burial chamber. Excavations were conducted from 1990 to 1993. The burial facility is a horizontal stone chamber orientated to the south. The chamber is two-tiered and is paved with flat pebbles. Inside were four Haji pottery coffins (2 tortoiseshell-shaped, 2 gabled house-shaped), wooden coffins with nails (1.8m in length, 0.6m in width), and an additional burial urn (medicine pot-shaped Sue pottery). Grave goods included small ball and cut-glass beads, weapons (iron swords, arrowheads), copper bowls, ax-shaped iron products, and Sue pottery (bowls, lids, and flat jars). Sue pottery jars have also been unearthed on both sides of the opening. The tumulus is estimated to have been built around the middle of the 7th century, at the end of the Kofun period.
;Ōya No.1 Kofun The Ōya No.1 Kofun was built on the southern slope of a hill overhanging the west bank of the Nakatsui River. The mound is rectangular, measuring 13.1 meters east–west, 9.8 meters north–south, and 3.8 meters high. It is surrounded by three tiers of stone guards, and two tiers of stones (18–22 meters in length) in front of the mound, giving the front view the appearance of a five-tier construction.The top of the mound is covered with flat stones. Excavations were conducted in 1988 and from 1992 to 1994. The burial facility is a horizontal stone chamber with two sleeves and opens in the south-southeast direction. The burial chamber was made from five megalithic stones, one each on the back wall, east wall, and ceiling, and two on the west wall. Inside was a Sue pottery ceramic coffin shaped like a house and a wooden coffin. Although the burial chamber had been robbed in antiquity, a number of grave goods were found, including a pair of dragon hilt swords, arrowheads, and arrowhead-shaped gilt bronze products and both Sue pottery and Haji pottery pottery. The excavated hilt swords are an important cultural property designated by Okayama Prefecture.<ref name="金銅装環頭大刀">[https://www.pref.okayama.jp/uploaded/attachment/261938.pdf 金銅装環頭大刀]（岡山県ホームページ）</ref> The tumulus is estimated to have been built around the end of the Kofun period around the latter half of the 7th century. Although it is not clear who was buried, there is a theory that he was Prince Ishikawa no Okimi, who died in 672, who played a role in the {{ill|Jinshin War|en|Jinshin War|WD=Q2378252|lt=Jinshin War}} and was recognized as ruler of Kibi around this time.
;Sada No.4 Kofun The Sada No.4 Kofun was excavated in 2006. Measuring approximately 6.6 meters from north to south and 8.2 meters from east to west, it is a three-tiered square tumulus. No relics have been found in the stone chamber, but plate-shaped iron products have been excavated from the southern end of the research area. It is estimated to have been built between the end of the 7th century and the beginning of the 8th century, the end of the Kofun period, making it the newest and smallest Kofun in the cluster.
;Sada No.5 Kofun The Sada No.5 Kofun was also excavated in 2006. It is a two-tiered square Kofun measuring about 8 meters from north to south and about 10.4 meters from east to west. Sue pottery was found in the burial chamber. It is estimated to have been built in the latter half of the 7th century, at the end of the Kofun period.
Large chieftain tombs of the 6th century Kingdom of Kibi were built in the southern part of the Kibi region, but the northern region, which is an important transportation hub and rich in iron resources, came under Yamato political influence in the 7th century and the construction of large Kofuns in this region shows a shifting of political and economic power from the south to the north during this time. In addition, the Kodono Site, believed to be the ruins of a {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period}} ''kanga'' (district headquarters) are located nearby, and it ay have some connection to this tumuli cluster.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The site is about ten minutes by car from [[:en:Chugoku Expressway|Chugoku Expressway]] Hokubo IC.

A common feature is that all are {{nihongo|''{{ill|Square Kofun|lt=hōfun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}''|方墳|1=Square Kofun||}}-style rectangular Kofuns, with a stepped (pyramid) structure and a stone wall layer of outer stones.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>

[[Archaeological excavation]]s have unearthed gold-decorated iron swords and other items from the cut stone [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]]. It is believed to date from the late 7th century. The Sada Kofun cluster is located at the foot of the mountain 1.5 km east of the Ōya No. 1 Kofun, and consist of five tumuli. The Sada-Higashi and Sada-Nishi tumuli were once regarded as a single "two conjoined rectangles" type ({{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|lt=zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|lt=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/zenpō-kōhō-fun|13=User:Immanuelle/zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (前方後方墳)); however, subsequent research indicated that they are separate tumuli.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
;Sada-Higashi Kofun The Sada-Higashi Kofun is rectangular, measuring 25 meters north to south, 18 meters east to west, and 6 meters high. The mound is surrounded by two tiers of stones. Excavations were conducted from 1994 to 1997. The burial facility is a horizontal stone chamber orientated to the south. The total length of the burial chamber is 11.6 meters including the entry passage. Inside were four {{ill|Haji pottery|en|Haji pottery|WD=Q5639938|lt=Haji-style ceramic}} coffins (2 tortoise shell-shaped, 2 gabled house-shaped, 1 four-pillar house-shaped) and a nailed wooden coffin. Grave goods included cearrings, small beads, gold thread iron long swords, arrowheads, halberds, and metal fittings, horse harness and metal fittings, agricultural tools and {{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|WD=Q7634351|lt=Sue pottery}} pottery (bowls, lids, vases, jars). It is estimated to have been built around the end of the Kofun period, around the first half of the 7th century, and is the oldest among the cluster of ''kofun''.
;Sada-Nishi Kofu The Sada-Nishi Kofun was built adjacent to the west side of the Sada-Higashi Kofun on the southern foot of a hill overhanging the east bank of the Nakatsui River. The mound is rectangular, measuring 16 meters north to south, 14 meters east to west, and 4.5 meters high and is surrounded by two tiers of stones. Excavations were conducted in 1994–1997. The burial facility is a horizontal stone chamber orientated to the south. The total length of the burial chamber including the entry passage was 10.7 meters. Inside were six Haji pottery coffins (2 tortoiseshell-shaped, 4 gabled house-shaped) and nailed wooden coffins. Grave goods were similar to that of the Sada-Higashi Kofun, and date the construction to the end of the Kofun period, around the first half of the 7th century, and immediately following the Sada-Higashi Kofun.
;Sada-Kita Kofun The Sada-Kita Kofun is located about 70 meters north of the Sada-Higashi Kofun and Sada-Nishi Kofun on the southern slope of a hill overhanging the east bank of the Nakatsui River. The mound is rectangular, measuring 25.3 meters from north to south and 21 meters from east to west, and is surrounded by three tiers of outer guard stones; the middle tier measuring 1.8 meters contains the opening of the burial chamber. Excavations were conducted from 1990 to 1993. The burial facility is a horizontal stone chamber orientated to the south. The chamber is two-tiered and is paved with flat pebbles. Inside were four Haji pottery coffins (2 tortoiseshell-shaped, 2 gabled house-shaped), wooden coffins with nails (1.8m in length, 0.6m in width), and an additional burial urn (medicine pot-shaped Sue pottery). Grave goods included small ball and cut-glass beads, weapons (iron swords, arrowheads), copper bowls, ax-shaped iron products, and Sue pottery (bowls, lids, and flat jars). Sue pottery jars have also been unearthed on both sides of the opening. The tumulus is estimated to have been built around the middle of the 7th century, at the end of the Kofun period.
;Ōya No.1 Kofun The Ōya No.1 Kofun was built on the southern slope of a hill overhanging the west bank of the Nakatsui River. The mound is rectangular, measuring 13.1 meters east–west, 9.8 meters north–south, and 3.8 meters high. It is surrounded by three tiers of stone guards, and two tiers of stones (18–22 meters in length) in front of the mound, giving the front view the appearance of a five-tier construction.The top of the mound is covered with flat stones. Excavations were conducted in 1988 and from 1992 to 1994. The burial facility is a horizontal stone chamber with two sleeves and opens in the south-southeast direction. The burial chamber was made from five megalithic stones, one each on the back wall, east wall, and ceiling, and two on the west wall. Inside was a Sue pottery ceramic coffin shaped like a house and a wooden coffin. Although the burial chamber had been robbed in antiquity, a number of grave goods were found, including a pair of dragon hilt swords, arrowheads, and arrowhead-shaped gilt bronze products and both Sue pottery and Haji pottery pottery. The excavated hilt swords are an important cultural property designated by Okayama Prefecture.<ref name="金銅装環頭大刀">[https://www.pref.okayama.jp/uploaded/attachment/261938.pdf 金銅装環頭大刀]（岡山県ホームページ）</ref> The tumulus is estimated to have been built around the end of the Kofun period around the latter half of the 7th century. Although it is not clear who was buried, there is a theory that he was Prince Ishikawa no Okimi, who died in 672, who played a role in the {{ill|Jinshin War|en|Jinshin War|WD=Q2378252|lt=Jinshin War}} and was recognized as ruler of Kibi around this time.
;Sada No.4 Kofun The Sada No.4 Kofun was excavated in 2006. Measuring approximately 6.6 meters from north to south and 8.2 meters from east to west, it is a three-tiered square tumulus. No relics have been found in the stone chamber, but plate-shaped iron products have been excavated from the southern end of the research area. It is estimated to have been built between the end of the 7th century and the beginning of the 8th century, the end of the Kofun period, making it the newest and smallest Kofun in the cluster.
;Sada No.5 Kofun The Sada No.5 Kofun was also excavated in 2006. It is a two-tiered square Kofun measuring about 8 meters from north to south and about 10.4 meters from east to west. Sue pottery was found in the burial chamber. It is estimated to have been built in the latter half of the 7th century, at the end of the Kofun period.
Large chieftain tombs of the 6th century Kingdom of Kibi were built in the southern part of the Kibi region, but the northern region, which is an important transportation hub and rich in iron resources, came under Yamato political influence in the 7th century and the construction of large Kofuns in this region shows a shifting of political and economic power from the south to the north during this time. In addition, the Kodono Site, believed to be the ruins of a {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period}} ''kanga'' (district headquarters) are located nearby, and it ay have some connection to this tumuli cluster.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The site is about ten minutes by car from [[:en:Chugoku Expressway|Chugoku Expressway]] Hokubo IC.

The tumuli were collectively designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 2008.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/140557|title=大谷・定古墳群|trans-title=Ōya-Sada Kofun-gun|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 31, 2022}}</ref> The tumuli area considered to be representative of large chieftain tombs of the late Kofun period of the [[Kingdom of Kibi]].
==Background== The Ōya-Sada Kofun Cluster is located by the Nakatsui River, which flows through a basin surrounded by the Kibi Plateau, in southern Maniwa. A common feature is that all are {{nihongo|''{{ill|Square Kofun|lt=hōfun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}''|方墳|1=Square Kofun||}}-style rectangular Kofuns, with a stepped (pyramid) structure and a stone wall layer of outer stones.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
Ōya No.1 Kofun was built in the latter half of the 7th century. [[Archaeological excavation]]s have unearthed gold-decorated iron swords and other items from the cut stone [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]]. It is believed to date from the late 7th century. The Sada Kofun cluster is located at the foot of the mountain 1.5 km east of the Ōya No. 1 Kofun, and consist of five tumuli. The Sada-Higashi and Sada-Nishi tumuli were once regarded as a single "two conjoined rectangles" type ({{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|lt=zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|lt=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/zenpō-kōhō-fun|13=User:Immanuelle/zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (前方後方墳)); however, subsequent research indicated that they are separate tumuli.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
;Sada-Higashi Kofun The Sada-Higashi Kofun is rectangular, measuring 25 meters north to south, 18 meters east to west, and 6 meters high. The mound is surrounded by two tiers of stones. Excavations were conducted from 1994 to 1997. The burial facility is a horizontal stone chamber orientated to the south. The total length of the burial chamber is 11.6 meters including the entry passage. Inside were four {{ill|Haji pottery|en|Haji pottery|WD=Q5639938|lt=Haji-style ceramic}} coffins (2 tortoise shell-shaped, 2 gabled house-shaped, 1 four-pillar house-shaped) and a nailed wooden coffin. Grave goods included cearrings, small beads, gold thread iron long swords, arrowheads, halberds, and metal fittings, horse harness and metal fittings, agricultural tools and {{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|WD=Q7634351|lt=Sue pottery}} pottery (bowls, lids, vases, jars). It is estimated to have been built around the end of the Kofun period, around the first half of the 7th century, and is the oldest among the cluster of ''kofun''.
;Sada-Nishi Kofu The Sada-Nishi Kofun was built adjacent to the west side of the Sada-Higashi Kofun on the southern foot of a hill overhanging the east bank of the Nakatsui River. The mound is rectangular, measuring 16 meters north to south, 14 meters east to west, and 4.5 meters high and is surrounded by two tiers of stones. Excavations were conducted in 1994–1997. The burial facility is a horizontal stone chamber orientated to the south. The total length of the burial chamber including the entry passage was 10.7 meters. Inside were six Haji pottery coffins (2 tortoiseshell-shaped, 4 gabled house-shaped) and nailed wooden coffins. Grave goods were similar to that of the Sada-Higashi Kofun, and date the construction to the end of the Kofun period, around the first half of the 7th century, and immediately following the Sada-Higashi Kofun.
;Sada-Kita Kofun The Sada-Kita Kofun is located about 70 meters north of the Sada-Higashi Kofun and Sada-Nishi Kofun on the southern slope of a hill overhanging the east bank of the Nakatsui River. The mound is rectangular, measuring 25.3 meters from north to south and 21 meters from east to west, and is surrounded by three tiers of outer guard stones; the middle tier measuring 1.8 meters contains the opening of the burial chamber. Excavations were conducted from 1990 to 1993. The burial facility is a horizontal stone chamber orientated to the south. The chamber is two-tiered and is paved with flat pebbles. Inside were four Haji pottery coffins (2 tortoiseshell-shaped, 2 gabled house-shaped), wooden coffins with nails (1.8m in length, 0.6m in width), and an additional burial urn (medicine pot-shaped Sue pottery). Grave goods included small ball and cut-glass beads, weapons (iron swords, arrowheads), copper bowls, ax-shaped iron products, and Sue pottery (bowls, lids, and flat jars). Sue pottery jars have also been unearthed on both sides of the opening. The tumulus is estimated to have been built around the middle of the 7th century, at the end of the Kofun period.
;Ōya No.1 Kofun The Ōya No.1 Kofun was built on the southern slope of a hill overhanging the west bank of the Nakatsui River. The mound is rectangular, measuring 13.1 meters east–west, 9.8 meters north–south, and 3.8 meters high. It is surrounded by three tiers of stone guards, and two tiers of stones (18–22 meters in length) in front of the mound, giving the front view the appearance of a five-tier construction.The top of the mound is covered with flat stones. Excavations were conducted in 1988 and from 1992 to 1994. The burial facility is a horizontal stone chamber with two sleeves and opens in the south-southeast direction. The burial chamber was made from five megalithic stones, one each on the back wall, east wall, and ceiling, and two on the west wall. Inside was a Sue pottery ceramic coffin shaped like a house and a wooden coffin. Although the burial chamber had been robbed in antiquity, a number of grave goods were found, including a pair of dragon hilt swords, arrowheads, and arrowhead-shaped gilt bronze products and both Sue pottery and Haji pottery pottery. The excavated hilt swords are an important cultural property designated by Okayama Prefecture.<ref name="金銅装環頭大刀">[https://www.pref.okayama.jp/uploaded/attachment/261938.pdf 金銅装環頭大刀]（岡山県ホームページ）</ref> The tumulus is estimated to have been built around the end of the Kofun period around the latter half of the 7th century. Although it is not clear who was buried, there is a theory that he was Prince Ishikawa no Okimi, who died in 672, who played a role in the {{ill|Jinshin War|en|Jinshin War|WD=Q2378252|lt=Jinshin War}} and was recognized as ruler of Kibi around this time.
;Sada No.4 Kofun The Sada No.4 Kofun was excavated in 2006. Measuring approximately 6.6 meters from north to south and 8.2 meters from east to west, it is a three-tiered square tumulus. No relics have been found in the stone chamber, but plate-shaped iron products have been excavated from the southern end of the research area. It is estimated to have been built between the end of the 7th century and the beginning of the 8th century, the end of the Kofun period, making it the newest and smallest Kofun in the cluster.
;Sada No.5 Kofun The Sada No.5 Kofun was also excavated in 2006. It is a two-tiered square Kofun measuring about 8 meters from north to south and about 10.4 meters from east to west. Sue pottery was found in the burial chamber. It is estimated to have been built in the latter half of the 7th century, at the end of the Kofun period.
Large chieftain tombs of the 6th century Kingdom of Kibi were built in the southern part of the Kibi region, but the northern region, which is an important transportation hub and rich in iron resources, came under Yamato political influence in the 7th century and the construction of large Kofuns in this region shows a shifting of political and economic power from the south to the north during this time. In addition, the Kodono Site, believed to be the ruins of a {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period}} ''kanga'' (district headquarters) are located nearby, and it ay have some connection to this tumuli cluster.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The site is about ten minutes by car from [[:en:Chugoku Expressway|Chugoku Expressway]] Hokubo IC.

{{ill|Historic Site of Japan|WD=Q30834580}}; {{ill|historic site|WD=Q1081138}}| website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }}{{OSM location map | coord = {{Coord|34|56|28|N|133|37|43|E}} | zoom = 15 | float = right | width = 300| height =250 | caption = {{center|大谷・定古墳群分布図}} | scalemark = 60 | mark-coord1= {{Coord|34|56|29.37|N|133|37|52.90|E}} | label1=定東塚古墳 | label-pos1=right | label-color1=black | mark1=方墳.png | mark-size1=25 | mark-coord2= {{Coord|34|56|29.30|N|133|37|52.00|E}} | label2=定西塚古墳 | label-pos2=left | label-color2=black | mark2=方墳.png | mark-size2=25 | mark-coord3= {{Coord|34|56|31.45|N|133|37|52.52|E}} | label3=定北古墳 | label-pos3=top | label-color3=black | mark3=方墳.png | mark-size3=25 | mark-coord4= {{Coord|34|56|22.15|N|133|37|29.25|E}} | label4=大谷1号墳 | label-pos4=top | label-color4=black | mark4=方墳.png | mark-size4=25 }} [[File:Sada Higashizuka Kofun and Sada Nishizuka Kofun, funkyu.jpg|thumb|270px|right|Sada-Higashi and Sada-Nishi Kofun]] [[File:Sada Kita Kofun, funkyu-2.jpg|270px|right|thumb|Sada-Kita Kofun]] The {{nihongo|'''Ōya-Sada Kofun Cluster'''|大谷・定古墳群| Ōya-Sada Kofun-gun|}} is a group of six ''[[kofun]]'' [[Kofun]]s located in the Kaminakatsui neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Maniwa, Okayama|Maniwa]], {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|en|Okayama Prefecture|WD=Q132936|lt=Okayama Prefecture}}, in the {{ill|San'yō region|en|San'yō region|WD=Q398087|lt=San'yō region}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. The tumuli were collectively designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 2008.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/140557|title=大谷・定古墳群|trans-title=Ōya-Sada Kofun-gun|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 31, 2022}}</ref> The tumuli area considered to be representative of large chieftain tombs of the late Kofun period of the [[Kingdom of Kibi]].
==Background== The Ōya-Sada Kofun Cluster is located by the Nakatsui River, which flows through a basin surrounded by the Kibi Plateau, in southern Maniwa. A common feature is that all are {{nihongo|''{{ill|Square Kofun|lt=hōfun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}''|方墳|1=Square Kofun||}}-style rectangular Kofuns, with a stepped (pyramid) structure and a stone wall layer of outer stones.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
Ōya No.1 Kofun was built in the latter half of the 7th century. [[Archaeological excavation]]s have unearthed gold-decorated iron swords and other items from the cut stone [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]]. It is believed to date from the late 7th century. The Sada Kofun cluster is located at the foot of the mountain 1.5 km east of the Ōya No. 1 Kofun, and consist of five tumuli. The Sada-Higashi and Sada-Nishi tumuli were once regarded as a single "two conjoined rectangles" type ({{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|lt=zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|lt=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/zenpō-kōhō-fun|13=User:Immanuelle/zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (前方後方墳)); however, subsequent research indicated that they are separate tumuli.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
;Sada-Higashi Kofun The Sada-Higashi Kofun is rectangular, measuring 25 meters north to south, 18 meters east to west, and 6 meters high. The mound is surrounded by two tiers of stones. Excavations were conducted from 1994 to 1997. The burial facility is a horizontal stone chamber orientated to the south. The total length of the burial chamber is 11.6 meters including the entry passage. Inside were four {{ill|Haji pottery|en|Haji pottery|WD=Q5639938|lt=Haji-style ceramic}} coffins (2 tortoise shell-shaped, 2 gabled house-shaped, 1 four-pillar house-shaped) and a nailed wooden coffin. Grave goods included cearrings, small beads, gold thread iron long swords, arrowheads, halberds, and metal fittings, horse harness and metal fittings, agricultural tools and {{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|WD=Q7634351|lt=Sue pottery}} pottery (bowls, lids, vases, jars). It is estimated to have been built around the end of the Kofun period, around the first half of the 7th century, and is the oldest among the cluster of ''kofun''.
;Sada-Nishi Kofu The Sada-Nishi Kofun was built adjacent to the west side of the Sada-Higashi Kofun on the southern foot of a hill overhanging the east bank of the Nakatsui River. The mound is rectangular, measuring 16 meters north to south, 14 meters east to west, and 4.5 meters high and is surrounded by two tiers of stones. Excavations were conducted in 1994–1997. The burial facility is a horizontal stone chamber orientated to the south. The total length of the burial chamber including the entry passage was 10.7 meters. Inside were six Haji pottery coffins (2 tortoiseshell-shaped, 4 gabled house-shaped) and nailed wooden coffins. Grave goods were similar to that of the Sada-Higashi Kofun, and date the construction to the end of the Kofun period, around the first half of the 7th century, and immediately following the Sada-Higashi Kofun.
;Sada-Kita Kofun The Sada-Kita Kofun is located about 70 meters north of the Sada-Higashi Kofun and Sada-Nishi Kofun on the southern slope of a hill overhanging the east bank of the Nakatsui River. The mound is rectangular, measuring 25.3 meters from north to south and 21 meters from east to west, and is surrounded by three tiers of outer guard stones; the middle tier measuring 1.8 meters contains the opening of the burial chamber. Excavations were conducted from 1990 to 1993. The burial facility is a horizontal stone chamber orientated to the south. The chamber is two-tiered and is paved with flat pebbles. Inside were four Haji pottery coffins (2 tortoiseshell-shaped, 2 gabled house-shaped), wooden coffins with nails (1.8m in length, 0.6m in width), and an additional burial urn (medicine pot-shaped Sue pottery). Grave goods included small ball and cut-glass beads, weapons (iron swords, arrowheads), copper bowls, ax-shaped iron products, and Sue pottery (bowls, lids, and flat jars). Sue pottery jars have also been unearthed on both sides of the opening. The tumulus is estimated to have been built around the middle of the 7th century, at the end of the Kofun period.
;Ōya No.1 Kofun The Ōya No.1 Kofun was built on the southern slope of a hill overhanging the west bank of the Nakatsui River. The mound is rectangular, measuring 13.1 meters east–west, 9.8 meters north–south, and 3.8 meters high. It is surrounded by three tiers of stone guards, and two tiers of stones (18–22 meters in length) in front of the mound, giving the front view the appearance of a five-tier construction.The top of the mound is covered with flat stones. Excavations were conducted in 1988 and from 1992 to 1994. The burial facility is a horizontal stone chamber with two sleeves and opens in the south-southeast direction. The burial chamber was made from five megalithic stones, one each on the back wall, east wall, and ceiling, and two on the west wall. Inside was a Sue pottery ceramic coffin shaped like a house and a wooden coffin. Although the burial chamber had been robbed in antiquity, a number of grave goods were found, including a pair of dragon hilt swords, arrowheads, and arrowhead-shaped gilt bronze products and both Sue pottery and Haji pottery pottery. The excavated hilt swords are an important cultural property designated by Okayama Prefecture.<ref name="金銅装環頭大刀">[https://www.pref.okayama.jp/uploaded/attachment/261938.pdf 金銅装環頭大刀]（岡山県ホームページ）</ref> The tumulus is estimated to have been built around the end of the Kofun period around the latter half of the 7th century. Although it is not clear who was buried, there is a theory that he was Prince Ishikawa no Okimi, who died in 672, who played a role in the {{ill|Jinshin War|en|Jinshin War|WD=Q2378252|lt=Jinshin War}} and was recognized as ruler of Kibi around this time.
;Sada No.4 Kofun The Sada No.4 Kofun was excavated in 2006. Measuring approximately 6.6 meters from north to south and 8.2 meters from east to west, it is a three-tiered square tumulus. No relics have been found in the stone chamber, but plate-shaped iron products have been excavated from the southern end of the research area. It is estimated to have been built between the end of the 7th century and the beginning of the 8th century, the end of the Kofun period, making it the newest and smallest Kofun in the cluster.
;Sada No.5 Kofun The Sada No.5 Kofun was also excavated in 2006. It is a two-tiered square Kofun measuring about 8 meters from north to south and about 10.4 meters from east to west. Sue pottery was found in the burial chamber. It is estimated to have been built in the latter half of the 7th century, at the end of the Kofun period.
Large chieftain tombs of the 6th century Kingdom of Kibi were built in the southern part of the Kibi region, but the northern region, which is an important transportation hub and rich in iron resources, came under Yamato political influence in the 7th century and the construction of large Kofuns in this region shows a shifting of political and economic power from the south to the north during this time. In addition, the Kodono Site, believed to be the ruins of a {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period}} ''kanga'' (district headquarters) are located nearby, and it ay have some connection to this tumuli cluster.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The site is about ten minutes by car from [[:en:Chugoku Expressway|Chugoku Expressway]] Hokubo IC.

{{ill|Historic Site of Japan|WD=Q30834580}}; {{ill|historic site|WD=Q1081138}}| website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }}{{OSM location map | coord = {{Coord|34|56|28|N|133|37|43|E}} | zoom = 15 | float = right | width = 300| height =250 | caption = {{center|大谷・定古墳群分布図}} | scalemark = 60 | mark-coord1= {{Coord|34|56|29.37|N|133|37|52.90|E}} | label1=定東塚古墳 | label-pos1=right | label-color1=black | mark1=方墳.png | mark-size1=25 | mark-coord2= {{Coord|34|56|29.30|N|133|37|52.00|E}} | label2=定西塚古墳 | label-pos2=left | label-color2=black | mark2=方墳.png | mark-size2=25 | mark-coord3= {{Coord|34|56|31.45|N|133|37|52.52|E}} | label3=定北古墳 | label-pos3=top | label-color3=black | mark3=方墳.png | mark-size3=25 | mark-coord4= {{Coord|34|56|22.15|N|133|37|29.25|E}} | label4=大谷1号墳 | label-pos4=top | label-color4=black | mark4=方墳.png | mark-size4=25 }} [[File:Sada Higashizuka Kofun and Sada Nishizuka Kofun, funkyu.jpg|thumb|270px|right|Sada-Higashi and Sada-Nishi Kofun]] [[File:Sada Kita Kofun, funkyu-2.jpg|270px|right|thumb|Sada-Kita Kofun]] The {{nihongo|'''Ōya-Sada Kofun Cluster'''|大谷・定古墳群| Ōya-Sada Kofun-gun|}} is a group of six ''[[kofun]]'' [[Kofun]]s located in the Kaminakatsui neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Maniwa, Okayama|Maniwa]], {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|en|Okayama Prefecture|WD=Q132936|lt=Okayama Prefecture}}, in the {{ill|San'yō region|en|San'yō region|WD=Q398087|lt=San'yō region}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. The tumuli were collectively designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 2008.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/140557|title=大谷・定古墳群|trans-title=Ōya-Sada Kofun-gun|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 31, 2022}}</ref> The tumuli area considered to be representative of large chieftain tombs of the late Kofun period of the [[Kingdom of Kibi]].
==Background== The Ōya-Sada Kofun Cluster is located by the Nakatsui River, which flows through a basin surrounded by the Kibi Plateau, in southern Maniwa. A common feature is that all are {{nihongo|''{{ill|Square Kofun|lt=hōfun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}''|方墳|1=Square Kofun||}}-style rectangular Kofuns, with a stepped (pyramid) structure and a stone wall layer of outer stones.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
Ōya No.1 Kofun was built in the latter half of the 7th century. [[Archaeological excavation]]s have unearthed gold-decorated iron swords and other items from the cut stone [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]]. It is believed to date from the late 7th century. The Sada Kofun cluster is located at the foot of the mountain 1.5 km east of the Ōya No. 1 Kofun, and consist of five tumuli. The Sada-Higashi and Sada-Nishi tumuli were once regarded as a single "two conjoined rectangles" type ({{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|lt=zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|lt=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/zenpō-kōhō-fun|13=User:Immanuelle/zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (前方後方墳)); however, subsequent research indicated that they are separate tumuli.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
;Sada-Higashi Kofun The Sada-Higashi Kofun is rectangular, measuring 25 meters north to south, 18 meters east to west, and 6 meters high. The mound is surrounded by two tiers of stones. Excavations were conducted from 1994 to 1997. The burial facility is a horizontal stone chamber orientated to the south. The total length of the burial chamber is 11.6 meters including the entry passage. Inside were four {{ill|Haji pottery|en|Haji pottery|WD=Q5639938|lt=Haji-style ceramic}} coffins (2 tortoise shell-shaped, 2 gabled house-shaped, 1 four-pillar house-shaped) and a nailed wooden coffin. Grave goods included cearrings, small beads, gold thread iron long swords, arrowheads, halberds, and metal fittings, horse harness and metal fittings, agricultural tools and {{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|WD=Q7634351|lt=Sue pottery}} pottery (bowls, lids, vases, jars). It is estimated to have been built around the end of the Kofun period, around the first half of the 7th century, and is the oldest among the cluster of ''kofun''.
;Sada-Nishi Kofu The Sada-Nishi Kofun was built adjacent to the west side of the Sada-Higashi Kofun on the southern foot of a hill overhanging the east bank of the Nakatsui River. The mound is rectangular, measuring 16 meters north to south, 14 meters east to west, and 4.5 meters high and is surrounded by two tiers of stones. Excavations were conducted in 1994–1997. The burial facility is a horizontal stone chamber orientated to the south. The total length of the burial chamber including the entry passage was 10.7 meters. Inside were six Haji pottery coffins (2 tortoiseshell-shaped, 4 gabled house-shaped) and nailed wooden coffins. Grave goods were similar to that of the Sada-Higashi Kofun, and date the construction to the end of the Kofun period, around the first half of the 7th century, and immediately following the Sada-Higashi Kofun.
;Sada-Kita Kofun The Sada-Kita Kofun is located about 70 meters north of the Sada-Higashi Kofun and Sada-Nishi Kofun on the southern slope of a hill overhanging the east bank of the Nakatsui River. The mound is rectangular, measuring 25.3 meters from north to south and 21 meters from east to west, and is surrounded by three tiers of outer guard stones; the middle tier measuring 1.8 meters contains the opening of the burial chamber. Excavations were conducted from 1990 to 1993. The burial facility is a horizontal stone chamber orientated to the south. The chamber is two-tiered and is paved with flat pebbles. Inside were four Haji pottery coffins (2 tortoiseshell-shaped, 2 gabled house-shaped), wooden coffins with nails (1.8m in length, 0.6m in width), and an additional burial urn (medicine pot-shaped Sue pottery). Grave goods included small ball and cut-glass beads, weapons (iron swords, arrowheads), copper bowls, ax-shaped iron products, and Sue pottery (bowls, lids, and flat jars). Sue pottery jars have also been unearthed on both sides of the opening. The tumulus is estimated to have been built around the middle of the 7th century, at the end of the Kofun period.
;Ōya No.1 Kofun The Ōya No.1 Kofun was built on the southern slope of a hill overhanging the west bank of the Nakatsui River. The mound is rectangular, measuring 13.1 meters east–west, 9.8 meters north–south, and 3.8 meters high. It is surrounded by three tiers of stone guards, and two tiers of stones (18–22 meters in length) in front of the mound, giving the front view the appearance of a five-tier construction.The top of the mound is covered with flat stones. Excavations were conducted in 1988 and from 1992 to 1994. The burial facility is a horizontal stone chamber with two sleeves and opens in the south-southeast direction. The burial chamber was made from five megalithic stones, one each on the back wall, east wall, and ceiling, and two on the west wall. Inside was a Sue pottery ceramic coffin shaped like a house and a wooden coffin. Although the burial chamber had been robbed in antiquity, a number of grave goods were found, including a pair of dragon hilt swords, arrowheads, and arrowhead-shaped gilt bronze products and both Sue pottery and Haji pottery pottery. The excavated hilt swords are an important cultural property designated by Okayama Prefecture.<ref name="金銅装環頭大刀">[https://www.pref.okayama.jp/uploaded/attachment/261938.pdf 金銅装環頭大刀]（岡山県ホームページ）</ref> The tumulus is estimated to have been built around the end of the Kofun period around the latter half of the 7th century. Although it is not clear who was buried, there is a theory that he was Prince Ishikawa no Okimi, who died in 672, who played a role in the {{ill|Jinshin War|en|Jinshin War|WD=Q2378252|lt=Jinshin War}} and was recognized as ruler of Kibi around this time.
;Sada No.4 Kofun The Sada No.4 Kofun was excavated in 2006. Measuring approximately 6.6 meters from north to south and 8.2 meters from east to west, it is a three-tiered square tumulus. No relics have been found in the stone chamber, but plate-shaped iron products have been excavated from the southern end of the research area. It is estimated to have been built between the end of the 7th century and the beginning of the 8th century, the end of the Kofun period, making it the newest and smallest Kofun in the cluster.
;Sada No.5 Kofun The Sada No.5 Kofun was also excavated in 2006. It is a two-tiered square Kofun measuring about 8 meters from north to south and about 10.4 meters from east to west. Sue pottery was found in the burial chamber. It is estimated to have been built in the latter half of the 7th century, at the end of the Kofun period.
Large chieftain tombs of the 6th century Kingdom of Kibi were built in the southern part of the Kibi region, but the northern region, which is an important transportation hub and rich in iron resources, came under Yamato political influence in the 7th century and the construction of large Kofuns in this region shows a shifting of political and economic power from the south to the north during this time. In addition, the Kodono Site, believed to be the ruins of a {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period}} ''kanga'' (district headquarters) are located nearby, and it ay have some connection to this tumuli cluster.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The site is about ten minutes by car from [[:en:Chugoku Expressway|Chugoku Expressway]] Hokubo IC.
{| class="wikitable" style="text-align:center;background:#ffffff;white-space:nowrap;font-size:85%" |+ Ōya-Sada Kofun Cluster main tumuli !Area !name !type !Dimensions !Burial chamber !Sarcophagus !Date |- |rowspan=3|'''Sada'''||'''Sada-Higashi'''||''hōfun''||18 x 25m||Single horizontal stone chamber||Haji pottery coffin (4), wooden coffin||early 7th c |- |'''Sada-Nishi'''||''hōfun''||14-16m||Single horizontal stone chamber||Haji pottery coffin (6), wooden coffin||early 7th c |- |'''Sada-Kita'''||''hōfun''||21-25.3m||Double horizontal stone chamber||Haji clay pottery coffin (4), wooden coffin||mid 7th c |- |'''Oya'''||'''Ōya No.1 '''||''hōfun''||9.8-13.1m (22m)||Double horizontal stone chamber||Sue clay pottery coffin (1), wooden coffin||Late 7th c |}

It is estimated to have been built between the end of the 7th century and the beginning of the 8th century, the end of the Kofun period, making it the newest and smallest Kofun in the cluster.
;Sada No.5 Kofun The Sada No.5 Kofun was also excavated in 2006. It is a two-tiered square Kofun measuring about 8 meters from north to south and about 10.4 meters from east to west. Sue pottery was found in the burial chamber. It is estimated to have been built in the latter half of the 7th century, at the end of the Kofun period.
Large chieftain tombs of the 6th century Kingdom of Kibi were built in the southern part of the Kibi region, but the northern region, which is an important transportation hub and rich in iron resources, came under Yamato political influence in the 7th century and the construction of large Kofuns in this region shows a shifting of political and economic power from the south to the north during this time. In addition, the Kodono Site, believed to be the ruins of a {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period}} ''kanga'' (district headquarters) are located nearby, and it ay have some connection to this tumuli cluster.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The site is about ten minutes by car from [[:en:Chugoku Expressway|Chugoku Expressway]] Hokubo IC.

{{Short description|Kofun period Kofun cluster in Maniwa, Okayama, Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site | name = Ōya-Sada Kofun Cluster | native_name =大谷・定古墳群 | native_name_lang = | image =Oya Kofun Group-1, funkyu-1.jpg; File:Oya Kofun Group-1, funkyu-1.jpg| image_size = | alt = | caption = Ōya No.1Kofun | map_type = Japan Okayama Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Ōya-Sada Kofun Cluster | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{Coord|34|56|22|N|133|37|29|E|region:JP-33_type:landmark|display=inline}} | map_dot_label = | location =[[:en:Maniwa, Okayama|Maniwa, Okayama]], {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}; {{ill|Maniwa|WD=Q861230|1=Maniwa}}| region = {{ill|San'yō region|en|San'yō region|WD=Q398087|lt=San'yō region|1=San'yō region}} | type = [[Kofun]] | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built =6th-7th century AD | abandoned = | epochs = [[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes | Historic Site =; {{ill|Historic Site of Japan|WD=Q30834580|1=Historic Site of Japan}}; {{ill|historic site|WD=Q1081138|1=Historic site}}| website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }}{{OSM location map | coord = {{Coord|34|56|28|N|133|37|43|E}} | zoom = 15 | float = right | width = 300| height =250 | caption = {{center|大谷・定古墳群分布図}} | scalemark = 60 | mark-coord1= {{Coord|34|56|29.37|N|133|37|52.90|E}} | label1=定東塚古墳 | label-pos1=right | label-color1=black | mark1=方墳.png | mark-size1=25 | mark-coord2= {{Coord|34|56|29.30|N|133|37|52.00|E}} | label2=定西塚古墳 | label-pos2=left | label-color2=black | mark2=方墳.png | mark-size2=25 | mark-coord3= {{Coord|34|56|31.45|N|133|37|52.52|E}} | label3=定北古墳 | label-pos3=top | label-color3=black | mark3=方墳.png | mark-size3=25 | mark-coord4= {{Coord|34|56|22.15|N|133|37|29.25|E}} | label4=大谷1号墳 | label-pos4=top | label-color4=black | mark4=方墳.png | mark-size4=25 }} [[File:Sada Higashizuka Kofun and Sada Nishizuka Kofun, funkyu.jpg|thumb|270px|right|Sada-Higashi and Sada-Nishi Kofun]] [[File:Sada Kita Kofun, funkyu-2.jpg|270px|right|thumb|Sada-Kita Kofun]] The {{nihongo|'''Ōya-Sada Kofun Cluster'''|大谷・定古墳群| Ōya-Sada Kofun-gun|}} is a group of six ''[[kofun]]'' [[Kofun]]s located in the Kaminakatsui neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Maniwa, Okayama|Maniwa]], {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|en|Okayama Prefecture|WD=Q132936|lt=Okayama Prefecture|1=Okayama Prefecture}}, in the {{ill|San'yō region|en|San'yō region|WD=Q398087|lt=San'yō region|1=San'yō region}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. The tumuli were collectively designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 2008.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/140557|title=大谷・定古墳群|trans-title=Ōya-Sada Kofun-gun|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 31, 2022}}</ref> The tumuli area considered to be representative of large chieftain tombs of the late Kofun period of the [[Kingdom of Kibi]].
==Background== The Ōya-Sada Kofun Cluster is located by the Nakatsui River, which flows through a basin surrounded by the Kibi Plateau, in southern Maniwa. A common feature is that all are {{nihongo|''{{ill|Square Kofun|lt=hōfun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}''|方墳|1=Square Kofun||}}-style rectangular Kofuns, with a stepped (pyramid) structure and a stone wall layer of outer stones.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
Ōya No.1 Kofun was built in the latter half of the 7th century. [[Archaeological excavation]]s have unearthed gold-decorated iron swords and other items from the cut stone [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]]. It is believed to date from the late 7th century. The Sada Kofun cluster is located at the foot of the mountain 1.5 km east of the Ōya No. 1 Kofun, and consist of five tumuli. The Sada-Higashi and Sada-Nishi tumuli were once regarded as a single "two conjoined rectangles" type ({{ill|zenpō-kōhō-fun|lt=zenpō-kōhō-fun|ja|前方後方墳|lt=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/zenpō-kōhō-fun|13=User:Immanuelle/zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (前方後方墳)); however, subsequent research indicated that they are separate tumuli.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
;Sada-Higashi Kofun The Sada-Higashi Kofun is rectangular, measuring 25 meters north to south, 18 meters east to west, and 6 meters high. The mound is surrounded by two tiers of stones. Excavations were conducted from 1994 to 1997. The burial facility is a horizontal stone chamber orientated to the south. The total length of the burial chamber is 11.6 meters including the entry passage. Inside were four {{ill|Haji pottery|en|Haji pottery|WD=Q5639938|lt=Haji-style ceramic|1=Haji pottery}} coffins (2 tortoise shell-shaped, 2 gabled house-shaped, 1 four-pillar house-shaped) and a nailed wooden coffin. Grave goods included cearrings, small beads, gold thread iron long swords, arrowheads, halberds, and metal fittings, horse harness and metal fittings, agricultural tools and {{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|WD=Q7634351|lt=Sue pottery|1=Sue pottery}} pottery (bowls, lids, vases, jars). It is estimated to have been built around the end of the Kofun period, around the first half of the 7th century, and is the oldest among the cluster of ''kofun''.
;Sada-Nishi Kofu The Sada-Nishi Kofun was built adjacent to the west side of the Sada-Higashi Kofun on the southern foot of a hill overhanging the east bank of the Nakatsui River. The mound is rectangular, measuring 16 meters north to south, 14 meters east to west, and 4.5 meters high and is surrounded by two tiers of stones. Excavations were conducted in 1994–1997. The burial facility is a horizontal stone chamber orientated to the south. The total length of the burial chamber including the entry passage was 10.7 meters. Inside were six Haji pottery coffins (2 tortoiseshell-shaped, 4 gabled house-shaped) and nailed wooden coffins. Grave goods were similar to that of the Sada-Higashi Kofun, and date the construction to the end of the Kofun period, around the first half of the 7th century, and immediately following the Sada-Higashi Kofun.
;Sada-Kita Kofun The Sada-Kita Kofun is located about 70 meters north of the Sada-Higashi Kofun and Sada-Nishi Kofun on the southern slope of a hill overhanging the east bank of the Nakatsui River. The mound is rectangular, measuring 25.3 meters from north to south and 21 meters from east to west, and is surrounded by three tiers of outer guard stones; the middle tier measuring 1.8 meters contains the opening of the burial chamber. Excavations were conducted from 1990 to 1993. The burial facility is a horizontal stone chamber orientated to the south. The chamber is two-tiered and is paved with flat pebbles. Inside were four Haji pottery coffins (2 tortoiseshell-shaped, 2 gabled house-shaped), wooden coffins with nails (1.8m in length, 0.6m in width), and an additional burial urn (medicine pot-shaped Sue pottery). Grave goods included small ball and cut-glass beads, weapons (iron swords, arrowheads), copper bowls, ax-shaped iron products, and Sue pottery (bowls, lids, and flat jars). Sue pottery jars have also been unearthed on both sides of the opening. The tumulus is estimated to have been built around the middle of the 7th century, at the end of the Kofun period.
;Ōya No.1 Kofun The Ōya No.1 Kofun was built on the southern slope of a hill overhanging the west bank of the Nakatsui River. The mound is rectangular, measuring 13.1 meters east–west, 9.8 meters north–south, and 3.8 meters high. It is surrounded by three tiers of stone guards, and two tiers of stones (18–22 meters in length) in front of the mound, giving the front view the appearance of a five-tier construction.The top of the mound is covered with flat stones. Excavations were conducted in 1988 and from 1992 to 1994. The burial facility is a horizontal stone chamber with two sleeves and opens in the south-southeast direction. The burial chamber was made from five megalithic stones, one each on the back wall, east wall, and ceiling, and two on the west wall. Inside was a Sue pottery ceramic coffin shaped like a house and a wooden coffin. Although the burial chamber had been robbed in antiquity, a number of grave goods were found, including a pair of dragon hilt swords, arrowheads, and arrowhead-shaped gilt bronze products and both Sue pottery and Haji pottery pottery. The excavated hilt swords are an important cultural property designated by Okayama Prefecture.<ref name="金銅装環頭大刀">[https://www.pref.okayama.jp/uploaded/attachment/261938.pdf 金銅装環頭大刀]（岡山県ホームページ）</ref> The tumulus is estimated to have been built around the end of the Kofun period around the latter half of the 7th century. Although it is not clear who was buried, there is a theory that he was Prince Ishikawa no Okimi, who died in 672, who played a role in the {{ill|Jinshin War|en|Jinshin War|WD=Q2378252|lt=Jinshin War|1=Jinshin War}} and was recognized as ruler of Kibi around this time.
;Sada No.4 Kofun The Sada No.4 Kofun was excavated in 2006. Measuring approximately 6.6 meters from north to south and 8.2 meters from east to west, it is a three-tiered square tumulus. No relics have been found in the stone chamber, but plate-shaped iron products have been excavated from the southern end of the research area. It is estimated to have been built between the end of the 7th century and the beginning of the 8th century, the end of the Kofun period, making it the newest and smallest Kofun in the cluster.
;Sada No.5 Kofun The Sada No.5 Kofun was also excavated in 2006. It is a two-tiered square Kofun measuring about 8 meters from north to south and about 10.4 meters from east to west. Sue pottery was found in the burial chamber. It is estimated to have been built in the latter half of the 7th century, at the end of the Kofun period.
Large chieftain tombs of the 6th century Kingdom of Kibi were built in the southern part of the Kibi region, but the northern region, which is an important transportation hub and rich in iron resources, came under Yamato political influence in the 7th century and the construction of large Kofuns in this region shows a shifting of political and economic power from the south to the north during this time. In addition, the Kodono Site, believed to be the ruins of a {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period}} ''kanga'' (district headquarters) are located nearby, and it ay have some connection to this tumuli cluster.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The site is about ten minutes by car from [[:en:Chugoku Expressway|Chugoku Expressway]] Hokubo IC.

It is estimated to have been built around the end of the Kofun period, around the first half of the 7th century, and is the oldest among the cluster of ''kofun''.

Inside were four Haji pottery coffins (2 tortoiseshell-shaped, 2 gabled house-shaped), wooden coffins with nails (1.8m in length, 0.6m in width), and an additional burial urn (medicine pot-shaped Sue pottery). Grave goods included small ball and cut-glass beads, weapons (iron swords, arrowheads), copper bowls, ax-shaped iron products, and Sue pottery (bowls, lids, and flat jars). Sue pottery jars have also been unearthed on both sides of the opening. The tumulus is estimated to have been built around the middle of the 7th century, at the end of the Kofun period.
;Ōya No.1 Kofun The Ōya No.1 Kofun was built on the southern slope of a hill overhanging the west bank of the Nakatsui River. The mound is rectangular, measuring 13.1 meters east–west, 9.8 meters north–south, and 3.8 meters high. It is surrounded by three tiers of stone guards, and two tiers of stones (18–22 meters in length) in front of the mound, giving the front view the appearance of a five-tier construction.The top of the mound is covered with flat stones. Excavations were conducted in 1988 and from 1992 to 1994. The burial facility is a horizontal stone chamber with two sleeves and opens in the south-southeast direction. The burial chamber was made from five megalithic stones, one each on the back wall, east wall, and ceiling, and two on the west wall. Inside was a Sue pottery ceramic coffin shaped like a house and a wooden coffin. Although the burial chamber had been robbed in antiquity, a number of grave goods were found, including a pair of dragon hilt swords, arrowheads, and arrowhead-shaped gilt bronze products and both Sue pottery and Haji pottery pottery. The excavated hilt swords are an important cultural property designated by Okayama Prefecture.<ref name="金銅装環頭大刀">[https://www.pref.okayama.jp/uploaded/attachment/261938.pdf 金銅装環頭大刀]（岡山県ホームページ）</ref> The tumulus is estimated to have been built around the end of the Kofun period around the latter half of the 7th century. Although it is not clear who was buried, there is a theory that he was Prince Ishikawa no Okimi, who died in 672, who played a role in the {{ill|Jinshin War|en|Jinshin War|WD=Q2378252|lt=Jinshin War|1=Jinshin War}} and was recognized as ruler of Kibi around this time.
;Sada No.4 Kofun The Sada No.4 Kofun was excavated in 2006. Measuring approximately 6.6 meters from north to south and 8.2 meters from east to west, it is a three-tiered square tumulus. No relics have been found in the stone chamber, but plate-shaped iron products have been excavated from the southern end of the research area. It is estimated to have been built between the end of the 7th century and the beginning of the 8th century, the end of the Kofun period, making it the newest and smallest Kofun in the cluster.
;Sada No.5 Kofun The Sada No.5 Kofun was also excavated in 2006. It is a two-tiered square Kofun measuring about 8 meters from north to south and about 10.4 meters from east to west. Sue pottery was found in the burial chamber. It is estimated to have been built in the latter half of the 7th century, at the end of the Kofun period.
Large chieftain tombs of the 6th century Kingdom of Kibi were built in the southern part of the Kibi region, but the northern region, which is an important transportation hub and rich in iron resources, came under Yamato political influence in the 7th century and the construction of large Kofuns in this region shows a shifting of political and economic power from the south to the north during this time. In addition, the Kodono Site, believed to be the ruins of a {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period}} ''kanga'' (district headquarters) are located nearby, and it ay have some connection to this tumuli cluster.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
The site is about ten minutes by car from [[:en:Chugoku Expressway|Chugoku Expressway]] Hokubo IC.
